points.text: Enable request‑level tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry) to correlate gateway 502/504 with downstream latency.
Collect gateway logs for error codes and timestamps; match them to backend logs via request IDs.
Measure per‑service response times; identify any downstream call exceeding its SLA.
Profile database CPU and query execution plans; look for slow queries or missing indexes.
Check DB connection pool usage; ensure pool size isn’t exhausted causing timeouts.
Add timeouts and circuit‑breaker thresholds around downstream calls to fail fast.
Implement exponential backoff retries for transient downstream failures, respecting idempotency.
Monitor system metrics (CPU, memory, network) before and after the release to spot regressions.
If a specific service is overloaded, consider scaling it horizontally or adding caching.
Review recent code changes for added synchronous calls or reduced query batching that could increase load.
Check gateway logs for 502/504 timestamps and correlate with backend request IDs to locate failing paths.
Enable distributed tracing to measure latency of each downstream call and identify slow services.
Profile backend code to ensure per‑call timeout budgets fit within the gateway timeout window.
Add circuit breakers around downstream services to fail fast and prevent cascading timeouts.
Analyze DB CPU spikes: examine query plans, add missing indexes, and tune connection pool size.
Limit parallelism or batch requests to the database to reduce contention and CPU load.
Rollback or feature‑flag recent changes to isolate the release that introduced the issue.
Run load tests replicating traffic patterns to reproduce the intermittent failures.
Adjust gateway timeout or retry policy if downstream latency cannot be reduced further.
Enable pg_stat_statements to identify top CPU‑consuming queries and their frequency.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the slow queries to see full scans, join types, and I/O costs.
Add missing indexes on columns used in WHERE clauses or JOIN conditions of the heavy queries.
Check pg_locks and deadlock logs for lock contention that could inflate CPU usage.
Tune the connection pool size to avoid too many concurrent sessions overwhelming the DB.
Instrument the application to measure latency of downstream calls vs DB calls to isolate the bottleneck.
Enable DB latency and lock‑wait metrics; identify the top CPU‑intensive queries.
Inspect execution plans of hot queries for full scans or missing indexes.
Monitor lock contention and deadlock rates; check isolation level impact.
Validate connection‑pool size and max connections to avoid DB thread exhaustion.
Profile downstream service calls; add circuit breakers or rate limiting to reduce load spikes.
Review recent schema migrations for new constraints that may cause row‑level locking.
Verify load balancer health checks are passing; failing checks cause 502.
Inspect LB request queue length and concurrency limits; high queue indicates overload.
Capture end‑to‑end latency from LB to backend; compare to timeout settings.
Enable detailed request tracing (e.g., X‑Request‑ID) to see which downstream call exceeds limits.
Monitor backend CPU, memory, and thread pool; high DB CPU may delay responses.
Check DB connection pool saturation and query execution times; increase pool or optimize queries.
Review gateway and backend timeout values; ensure they exceed worst‑case downstream latency.
Look for Linux socket errors (ECONNRESET, ETIMEDOUT) in /var/log/syslog to spot network drops.
Temporarily add circuit‑breaker or retry logic around slow downstream services to reduce 504 spikes.
Scale out backend or DB read replicas if CPU consistently high after release.
Enable TCP metrics (retransmits, RTT, RTO) on gateway and backend to detect congestion causing 502/504.
Verify keepalive and idle timeout settings to prevent premature connection closures at the gateway.
Inspect connection pool sizes for downstream calls; pool exhaustion can increase latency and trigger gateway errors.
Correlate database CPU spikes with TCP SYN retries or packet loss on the DB network path.
Capture packets between gateway and services to identify loss, high latency, or retransmission bursts.
If persistent retransmissions appear, consider tuning TCP congestion control or increasing socket buffers.
Measure gateway latency distribution (p95/p99) and error rates to establish baseline performance.
Enable distributed tracing to capture per‑call latency and failure rates for each downstream service.
Apply Little’s Law (R = λ·W) to estimate required concurrency given observed request rate and average service time.
Check database CPU utilization at p95 load; >80% suggests DB is the bottleneck needing scaling or query optimization.
Budget total request timeout by allocating time slices to each downstream call plus a safety margin.
If a downstream exceeds its timeout slice, add circuit breaking or reduce retries to prevent gateway 502/504 errors.
Run load‑test increasing RPS to verify that added DB capacity or adjusted timeouts eliminate intermittent failures.
